questions.q: In the MESI cache coherence protocol, what state does a cache line enter after a processor writes to a line it exclusively owns?
options: Modified (M) - the line is dirty and only this cache holds a valid copy
Shared (S) - the processor announces the write to other caches
Exclusive (E) - the line is marked clean but exclusively held
Invalid (I) - the written line must be invalidated immediately
questions.explanation: After a write to an Exclusive line, it transitions to Modified because it now contains data different from main memory. Only the modifying cache holds a valid copy. The other states represent non-exclusive or clean conditions, not the result of a local write.

questions.q: What is the primary difference between snooping and directory-based cache coherence protocols?
options: Snooping scales better to large processor counts; directory is simpler but limited
Directory protocols broadcast invalidations to all caches; snooping uses a centralized directory
Snooping uses broadcast on a shared bus; directory stores coherence state at a centralized directory
Snooping is used only in single-core processors; directory supports multicore
questions.explanation: Snooping coherence relies on a shared bus where all cache controllers observe (snoop) coherence transactions. Directory coherence maintains a directory that tracks which caches hold copies, enabling targeted invalidations without broadcast. This distinction determines scalability and traffic patterns.

questions.q: False sharing occurs when two processors modify different data elements that happen to reside on the same cache line. Which phenomenon results from false sharing?
options: Cache lines are unnecessarily invalidated and must be refetched, degrading performance
Both processors can write to the line simultaneously without conflict
The coherence protocol automatically merges the two data elements
Memory bandwidth is reduced but cache hit rates remain unchanged
questions.explanation: False sharing causes coherence traffic even though the processors are accessing different logical data. Each write by one processor invalidates the cache line in the other processor, forcing expensive reloads. This occurs despite no true data dependency, hence the performance penalty.

questions.q: In a MESI protocol, when a processor reads a line in the Shared state and then requests to write to it, what sequence of state transitions is required?
options: Shared → Modified directly
Shared → Exclusive → Modified
Shared → Invalid → Modified
Shared → Modified with broadcast invalidation
questions.explanation: From Shared, the processor cannot write directly because other caches may have copies. It must first acquire exclusive ownership (via an upgrade request), transitioning to Exclusive, then the write moves it to Modified. This prevents multiple writers and ensures coherence.

questions.q: A write-through cache policy in a multiprocessor system, without additional coherence mechanisms, can lead to which problem?
options: All writes miss because the cache capacity is exhausted
Stale data in other processors' caches because they don't see writes to main memory immediately
Writes become serialized and unscalable due to memory bus contention
Write-through doesn't cause problems; it ensures coherence automatically
questions.explanation: Write-through causes every write to access main memory, creating a bottleneck on the memory bus. While it does push data to memory, it doesn't explicitly invalidate other caches, and the bus contention severely limits scalability. Coherence protocols (write-back + invalidation) are more efficient.

questions.q: In the MESI state machine, what event causes a cache line to transition from Exclusive (E) to Shared (S)?
options: Another processor writes to the same line
Another processor reads the same line, and the directory allows it
The local processor reads the line again
The line is evicted from the cache
questions.explanation: When another processor requests a read to an Exclusively-held line, the directory or snooping mechanism can allow a shared copy. The line transitions to Shared in both caches. A write by another processor would invalidate it; a read is a shared-access event.

questions.q: What is a key limitation of snooping-based cache coherence that directory-based protocols address?
options: Snooping cannot detect write misses
Snooping requires broadcast on a shared bus, limiting scalability to roughly 16-32 processors
Directory protocols eliminate cache misses entirely
Snooping cannot maintain the Exclusive state
questions.explanation: Snooping requires all cache controllers to monitor a shared bus, which becomes a bottleneck and limits scalability. Directory protocols scale to hundreds or thousands of processors by using a centralized directory to target invalidations only to caches that hold copies.

questions.q: In a directory-based protocol, what information does the directory maintain for each cache line?
options: The content of the cache line (a full copy)
Which processors have copies of the line and in what coherence state
A prediction of which processors will read the line next
A write timestamp to order concurrent writes
questions.explanation: The directory tracks ownership and sharer information for each line, enabling it to send targeted invalidations or coherence messages. It doesn't store line data (that's in caches and memory) or predict future accesses.

questions.q: What is the primary reason that write invalidate is preferred over write update in cache coherence protocols?
options: Update requires writing to every copy; invalidate requires only invalidation messages
Update is faster for reads but slower for writes
Invalidate increases memory bandwidth usage
Update protocols have lower hardware complexity
questions.explanation: Write invalidate (MESI, MOESI) requires only invalidation messages to other copies, whereas write update requires broadcasting the new value to all caches holding the line. For workloads with multiple writers, invalidate is much more efficient and creates less traffic.

questions.q: In a system with hardware prefetching and write-invalidate coherence, false sharing between prefetched and written data can cause what problem?
options: Prefetched data becomes incoherent and corrupts results
Cache lines invalidated by writes trigger unnecessary prefetches, creating coherence thrashing
Prefetching is disabled automatically in coherent systems
The directory protocol prevents this by filtering prefetch requests
questions.explanation: If core A prefetches a line that core B subsequently writes to, the prefetch is wasted and the line is invalidated. If core A continues prefetching and core B continues writing the same line, pathological coherence traffic (thrashing) results. This is compounded by false sharing—they may not even access overlapping data.
